title: 23 Meskerem — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "23 Meskerem — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"23 Meskerem — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Meskerem

St. Solomon the King and Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

The Conception of St. John the Baptist

St. Xanthippe the Apostolic

The twenty-third day of Meskerem belongs to St. Solomon the King in his monthly commemoration — and in the Ethiopian context, as has been noted through the previous months, Solomon carries a weight that no other Christian calendar can replicate. The Solomonic dynasty, the Kebra Nagast, the union with the Queen of Sheba, the birth of Menelik I and the bringing of the Ark to Ethiopia — these are not merely legendary narratives but the founding charter of Ethiopian Christian civilization, the story through which the Church understands its own divine election and its particular place in the economy of salvation. In Meskerem, the month of new beginnings, Solomon's monthly feast is a remembrance of origins — of the deep roots from which the tree of Ethiopian Christianity has grown across three thousand years of claimed continuity from the house of David.

The Conception of St. John the Baptist falls on the twenty-third of Meskerem — nine months before the Baptist's birthday on the twenty-fourth of Yekatit — and its placement in the calendar is an act of theological precision: the Church counts John's life from his conception, not from his birth, because the Gospel of Luke records that he leapt in the womb when Mary's greeting reached Elizabeth's ears. He was full of the Holy Spirit from the womb, as the angel had promised to his father Zechariah, and his prophetic vocation began before his birth. The Conception of John in Meskerem places the Forerunner's story at the very opening of the new year's narrative — the announcement that precedes all other announcements, the beginning of the beginning. In the post-Maskal month, with the Cross already blazing on the hilltops, the Conception of the one who would cry 'Behold the Lamb of God' is the year's most intimate preparation.

St. Xanthippe the Apostolic was a Spanish noblewoman who, according to tradition, was converted through the preaching of St. Paul and became one of the earliest and most committed members of the Western apostolic community. She is described as having shared in the apostolic mission with a zeal that placed her alongside the great women missionaries of the early Church — women whose stories the tradition has often preserved only partially, but whose contribution to the spread of the Gospel in the first generation was essential and irreplaceable. On the twenty-third day of Meskerem, Xanthippe's presence in the assembly is the Western Church's testimony that the Cross whose Ethiopian feast was celebrated on the seventeenth day belongs to Hispania as much as it belongs to the highlands of Shewa — that the trophy found by Helena is distributed to the entire Body.

Solomon, the Conception of John, and Xanthippe together on this twenty-third day weave the month's threads into a pattern of origins and preparations: the dynastic origin of the Church's deepest self-understanding, the conceived but unborn Forerunner who will one day cry out at the Jordan, and the noblewoman of the West who received the Cross's message from the apostle who carried it furthest. The new year is more than three weeks old, and Meskerem's golden light is maturing from the fresh brightness of Enkutatash into the richer, deeper glow of a month that has found its full identity.
